data.title: Trustworthy medical AI systems need to know when they don’t know

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1136/medethics-2021-107463?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1136/medethics-2021-107463, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: There is much to learn from Durán and Jongsma’s paper. One particularly important insight concerns the relationship between epistemology and ethics in medical artificial intelligence (AI). In clinical environments, the task of AI systems is to provide risk estimates or diagnostic decisions, which then need to be weighed by physicians. Hence, while the implementation of AI systems might give rise to ethical issues—for example, overtreatment, defensive medicine or paternalism—the issue that lies at the heart is an epistemic problem: how can physicians know whether to trust decisions made by AI systems? In this manner, various studies examining the interaction of AI systems and physicians have shown that without being able to evaluate their trustworthiness, especially novice physicians become overreliant on algorithmic support—and ultimately are led astray by incorrect decisions. This leads to a second insight from the paper, namely that even if some (deep learningbased) AI system happens to be opaque, it is still not built on the moon. To assess its trustworthiness, AI developers or physicians have different sorts of higher order evidence at hand. Most importantly, before an AI system is being implemented into clinical settings, it is being validated. The validation yields evidence of a system’s accuracy and reliability. In this respect, Durán’s account of ‘computational reliability’ proves to be a useful starting point to evaluate the trustworthiness of AI systems. However, while the individual criteria can all be considered as necessary conditions, they are not sufficient to clinch an AI system’s trustworthiness. To make this clear, let us consider them in detail: validating an AI system is of course a standard procedure, with the performance usually being measured in a benchmark data set. Especially for deep learningbased AI systems, there are serious concerns about the extent to which traditional validation methods establish the external validity of AI systems: many of them achieve high predictive accuracy by exploiting short cuts. Hence, as Durán and Jongma emphasise, the validation needs to be supplemented by some sort of robustness analysis. For instance, the algorithm might be shown data outside of training conditions—for example, medical images made by nonstandard devices. That said, it is not fully clear to me, how conditions ‘a history of (un)successful implementations’ and ‘expert knowledge’ ought to be interpreted. Within the context of clinical medicine, I assume that both conditions state that the track record and best practices in using some AI system should be considered. The problem with the account of ‘computational reliability’ is that it tells physicians something about the general capacities of an AI system, but nothing about their reliability in individual situations. This is important insofar as many diagnostic situations are inherently uncertain. Take the example of an imagebased diagnostic system: the signs and symptoms in an image may be ambiguous, the image might have been taken with a nonstandard device, some data might be missing in the image and there is high intervariability in how physicians diagnose and grade diseases. In conjunction, these factors make it difficult to straightforwardly infer the trustworthiness of an individual algorithmic diagnosis from the general capacities of an AI system. Needless to say, within the context of clinical medicine, single incorrect decisions can have devastating consequences. Physicians thus need to be in an epistemic position to evaluate when to abstain from decisions made by the AI system. To ground the trust in AI systems, it is not necessary to open up the black box—even though it is certainly helpful if a physician can understand what signs and symptoms an AI system has focused its attention on through some post hoc explanation. Rather, a missing element
